exemplar.title: M25 #2: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #2: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained focus on the prescribed title with clear, coherent argumentation throughout.
Effective use of relevant, specific examples (Manetâs Olympia, linear perspective, intron discovery, Twin Paradox) that are woven into the analysis rather than presented as isolated anecdotes.
Demonstrates awareness of alternative perspectives by contrasting interpretive versus technical knowledge in the arts and empirical versus theoretical knowledge in the natural sciences.
weaknesses: Evaluation of opposing positions remains at a surface level; counter-arguments are asserted rather than deeply probed.
Some examples (notably the intron case and aspects of the twin paradox) lack critical depth and methodological analysis.
Transitions between sections (especially between the arts and sciences) rely on assertions rather than fully reasoned signposting, slightly weakening overall coherence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The official examiner award of 7 reflects a performance solidly within the 7â8 band but at its lower boundary. The essay sustains a clear and consistent focus on the title, deploys relevant examples effectively, and shows awareness of alternative viewpoints, meriting credit in the upper bands of the rubric. However, the critical evaluation of counter-arguments is underdeveloped, some examples are treated descriptively rather than analytically, and the coherence of transitions is uneven. These limitations prevent placement in the top half of the band. Allocating all 7 available marks to the single gradable criterion (Understanding Knowledge Questions) aligns with the examinerâs total, accurately reflecting the essayâs demonstrated strengths and acknowledged weaknesses.

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively situates the fragility of knowledge in both arts and sciences by highlighting human fallibility, establishing a clear context for the discussion.
The definition of ârevered knowledgeâ is broad and descriptive; refine it to specify criteria for fragility and relate directly to your essayâs central argument.
The opening question is rhetorical but does not immediately connect to your thesis; consider rephrasing to present a more precise claim about knowledge fragility.
The sentence ending with âpeople may take everything theyâ is incomplete; ensure each claim is fully articulated to maintain argumentative coherence.
The term âinerrantâ is misused when describing paradoxes; replace it with precise terminology such as âunfalsifiableâ or âconceptually robust.â
You note experimental confirmation of time dilation, yet claim paradoxes cannot be tested; reconcile this tension to clarify your argument.
Transitions between the arts and natural sciences are abrupt; add signposting to guide the reader through your comparative structure.
Saying linear perspective was âapproved by the audienceâ is vague; cite specific sources or reactions to demonstrate how consensus formed around this technique.
Your comparative paragraph conflates subjective and objective knowledge; clarify how each is produced differently and why both remain vulnerable.
Speculating that a single failed study could collapse our entire understanding is hyperbolic; ground this with historical cases where major theories were overturned.
The use of Manetâs Olympia provides a concrete historical example illustrating how interpretation and cultural context shape the perceived value and fragility of artworks.
Introducing an early example from the arts alongside Barthesâ theory would strengthen your argument by illustrating fragility in practice rather than theory alone.
You cite Barthesâ âThe Death of the Authorâ but do not link it back to your definition of revered knowledge; explicitly explain how decentralizing authorship increases fragility.
In discussing Olympiaâs initial scandal, you describe reactions without linking them back to fragility; explain how changing moral standards undermine stability of artistic knowledge.
The transition to foundational techniques feels abrupt; include a linking sentence summarizing why technique stability contrasts with interpretive fragility.
Asserting that technical knowledge is âless subject to changeâ overlooks historical shifts; qualify this claim by acknowledging exceptions in artistic practice.
Your analysis of Gombrichâs test of time is descriptive; deepen the evaluation by considering alternative readings or criticisms of the theoryâs applicability to contemporary art.
The example of The Last Supper is valid but underdeveloped; analyze how its enduring use of perspective supports your argument about technique resilience.
The conclusion restates fragility but offers limited evaluation; draw clearer implications about how knowledge fragility affects decision making or ethics.
The account of intron discovery is chronological; improve critical depth by discussing methodological challenges or debates over the definition of âjunkâ DNA.
When referencing Zhang et al., identify specific experiments or data rather than generalizing about âregulatory functionsâ to strengthen your scientific analysis.
Explain why thought experiments like the twin paradox resist empirical testing, perhaps by discussing constraints on replicating relativistic velocities.
Linking Einsteinâs Nobel Prize to paradox validity conflates reputation with theoretical soundness; separate the two to strengthen your logic.
The essay begins by restating the title; consider an engaging thesis that outlines your unique perspective on knowledge fragility instead of mirroring the prompt.
While the word count meets requirements, the lengthy introduction limits depth in analysis; streamline early paragraphs to allocate space for deeper critique.
The Thomas Kuhn quote is truncated and contains typographical errors; correct the citation and explain its relevance to paradigm shifts in knowledge.
Your statement that âneither subjective nor objective knowledge makes it robustâ is unclear; refine to specify which elements contribute to resilience in each AOK.
The final synthesis is formulaic; strengthen your closing by directly tying back to your refined definition of revered knowledge from the introduction.

The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
